stories.title: The Glasswright's Rebellion
stories.text: In the Lampwick Quarter of Keshtir, where glass-makers had worked for three generations, Tavrin the Glasswright discovered that his craft could resist the Dominion's ledger-magic. The State's auditors seized his forge. Tavrin fled into the underground kilns, where he found a half-dozen apprentices hiding, their own consciousness already scattered by the Remaking's aftermath. Using starlight-glass salvaged from forbidden ruins, Tavrin taught them a counter-song: a melody older than kingdoms, sung through molten metal. The glass remembered. It sang back. Within a week, weapon-glass—recurved blades that held the memory of makers—began circulating through the shadow-markets of Keshtir. The Dominion couldn't audit it. Ledger-magic slid off its surface like water on oiled cloth. Tavrin stood in their heart, glass-dust jeweling his hair, and offered the Auditor a choice: burn the quarter and lose the knowledge of starlight-forging forever, or acknowledge that some crafts transcend the ledger. The Auditor left without burning anything. But she also didn't leave the quarter unscarred.

stories.title: The Drowned King's Bride
stories.text: Captain Vessa of Verdant Strand was a widow twice over when the Deepkin Keeper of Pearls offered her a bargain: marry the Drowned King in ritual, and the merfolk would gift Verdant Strand a decade of safe passage through the Sapphire Sea. No war. No toll. No drowning of merchant vessels. Vessa accepted. The ceremony took place in the Coral Chamber, where she spoke vows in a language made of waves and sorrow. The Drowned King was no monster—ancient, yes, and grief-stricken beyond measure, but he wept when she took his hand. For three months, Vessa kept her half of the bargain: she sailed the abyss-boundary waters once per moon-cycle, singing to the King in the drowned cities, teaching him the names of her lost husbands and the taste of surface wine. But the Drowned God was waking. The Tear of the Drowned God frayed daily. On the night of the third new moon, the King asked her to stay below forever—not as wife, but as anchor. A willing sacrifice to seal the boundary.

stories.title: The Bone-Carver's Threshold
stories.text:
The Dominion's ledgers had erased her village—literally crossed it out as if it never existed—and Moriel carried that erasure like a wound. When Naela the bone-carver offered her apprenticeship, Moriel knew it was a test: could she carve the Carved Tongue into bone without losing herself to the language's hunger? For six weeks, Moriel learned. Naela taught her to listen to bone's memory—each fragment held the echo of an animal's fear, its mate-call, its final breath. To carve was to dialogue with those echoes, to ask the bone: "What do you wish to say?"

Moriel's first working was small: a prayer-bone inscribed for a dying merchant. The bone sang, and the merchant found peace. But the Carved Tongue had marked Moriel. Her fingers began to ache with the weight of words not-yet-written. She dreamed in syllables that predated human speech. On the night of the new moon, Naela brought Moriel to the Sunken Well—a place of transformation that bone-singers spoke of in hushed tones.

stories.title: The Warlord's Last Ledger
stories.text: Kethran had conquered three provinces through blood and terror. His war-band was legendary: the Ironbound, they called themselves, loyal beyond reason. But the Remaking had shattered gold into meaninglessness, and with it, Kethran's ability to pay his soldiers. On the morning he couldn't meet their wages, the Ironbound began to drift. Some died. Some deserted. Some turned to brigandage just to eat. Kethran rode into the Wychflow Valley alone, seeking a rival warlord named Brennus. He found Brennus in his fortress, surrounded by his own starving war-band, facing the same reckoning. Instead of fighting, Kethran did something unexpected: he proposed alliance. Not conquest. Not dominion. A shared shelter and shared stores, pooled until the world stabilized or they found other work. Brennus studied Kethran's face for lies and found none. Brennus burned his own fortress for its timber and stone. Together, they and Kethran rode to Pelimor and established a waystation for displaced soldiers—one that asked nothing but offered refuge. Some soldiers were rehired as guards. Some found peace in refugee camps. Some never forgave them.

stories.title: The Merchant's Shadow
stories.text: Kaiden was a merchant of Anthrim who discovered something precious in the Remaking's chaos: forgotten trades. While others scrambled for consciousness-fragments, Kaiden sought abandoned workshops, sealed libraries, forbidden looms. He would restore a single craft—teaching a widow the weaver's art her mother had lost, showing a young man how to forge bone-music into healing prayer-charms. Each apprentice became a carrier of knowledge the Dominion had tried to erase. But Kaiden was running out of time. The Dominion had begun hunting knowledge-keepers. On the night they came for him, Kaiden gathered his students in the back room—a dozen apprentices, each holding the seed of a dying craft. Instead of scattering, they linked hands. Kaiden began to hum, and the apprentices answered with the first songs of their disciplines. Loom-song and bone-music wove together into something neither wholly sound nor magic—a resonance that split the warehouse walls like paper. When the grey-cloaked collectors arrived, they found only dust and the smell of old wood-smoke. But across Anthrim, in hidden rooms and midnight gardens, the apprentices had begun to teach.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Reckoning
stories.text: The Wyvern was bound by ancient sorcery to devour the innocent every seventh moon—a curse laid by a dead mage whose betrayal the creature could never wash away. For decades it circled the mountain village of Ashenvale, waiting for the inevitable theft, the broken promise that would trigger the feeding. But Ashenvale's newest warden, Kess, studied the curse's architecture instead of building higher walls. She walked to the Wyvern's mountain alone, carrying rope and bread. Over three nights, she spoke with the creature—not bargaining, but listening. The Wyvern told her the mage's true name, the cause of the betrayal, the shape of the sorcery twisted into its blood. On the fourth dawn, Kess sang the reversal. The curse unwound like black thread burning. The Wyvern's eyes cleared—gold and ancient, no longer hunger-mad. It flew north, toward the mage's buried tomb, to complete a reckoning of its own. When it returned, it brought something precious: the mage's journal, which Kess used to free seventeen other cursed creatures.

stories.title: The Glassmaker's Defiance
stories.text: In Keshtir's Lampwick Quarter, where glass-forges had sung for three generations, Master Tavrin knelt before a cooling crucible. The Dominion's auditors had sealed his workshop seven days prior—confiscating his bioluminescent inks, his memory-vessels, the copper-and-bone mechanisms that let glass remember. The new tax was simple: all craft-work belonged to the state, payment in consciousness-fragments or refusal meant exile. Tavrin was sixty winters old. He had forged weapons-glass for warlords, memory-vessels for bone-singers, and once—a single time—a crown-mirror that showed a king the truth of his cruelty. He would not work for the Dominion. He broke the seals himself, using an old apprentice's trick: humming a frequency only glass could hear. The furnace roared back to life. By midnight, he had cast a single blade—not for war, but for cutting. Its edge could sever ledgers, the auditors' phantom-books that rewrote reality moment by moment. When the grey-cloaked Dominion collectors arrived at dawn, they found the workshop empty.

stories.title: The Tide-Bride's Bargain
stories.text: Kendra was a thief's daughter raised in Greyport's Saltmere docks, until the morning the merfolk captain found her stealing pearls from a drowned archive. Instead of drowning her, Captain Merith offered a wager: steal from the Dominion's harbor vault—where the Tear of the Ashglass rested—and Kendra would have her heart's desire. Fail, and she would become a tide-bride, bound to Merith's deepkin crew forever. Kendra accepted. She had always been clever with knots and locks, and besides, what more could she lose? The vault lay seven fathoms down, guarded by phantoms and pressure-currents that broke bones. But Merith taught her to breathe like the tide, to move through water as though it were memory itself. Three nights they trained together, and on the third, Kendra felt something unexpected: the captain's webbed hand finding her own in the dark, and Merith's song—a melody older than kingdoms—wrapping around her like recognition. They reached the vault. The Tear blazed there, a fragment of something dying and divine. Kendra's thief-hands shook. She thought of freedom, of the dry world above.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Last Map
stories.text:
Aldrin had inherited his grandmother's maps—nine perfect charts of a world that had been rewritten nine times. The Dominion paid handsomely for cartographers, but Aldrin knew the truth that got men killed: the maps weren't copies of reality. They were prophecies. The ninth map was incomplete. It showed a tenth iteration approaching—a world where consciousness and stone could merge, where geography would be sentient, where the boundary between map and terrain would dissolve. Aldrin was supposed to deliver all nine to the Dominion archives on a date certain. Instead, he walked to the Bleached Wastes and climbed the Rust Gate at dawn. Beyond lay the unmappable—the region where three versions of reality bled into one another, where compasses spun wild and landmarks forgot their own names. A bone-singer's apprentice found him three days into the wastes, half-mad, charcoal-stained, and frantic with work. "The tenth," Aldrin gasped, "cannot be drawn by hand. It has to be sung. The land has to sing itself into being."

The apprentice understood.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Mercy
stories.text: The Wyvern had been caged in crystal for a hundred years by a sorcerer-king whose cruelty it had finally refused. When the Remaking shattered the cage, the creature emerged starving and half-mad, drawn toward the nearest village—Thornhearth—by the scent of livestock and fear. But the village's healer, a woman named Sorenne, walked to the creature unarmed. She carried only a bowl of fresh water and the knowledge that the Wyvern's fury was not its nature but its wound. Over weeks, Sorenne tended the creature—bathing its crystallized scales, singing old songs that eased its nightmares. The Wyvern learned that gentleness was possible. When traders came to trap it for the new regime, Sorenne stood in the creature's shadow. The Wyvern flew her north, to the Counter-Archive, where she became its liaison—translating between dragonkind and human resistance. The creature never returned to Thornhearth. But each winter, it left fresh game at the village's edge, and the children grew up knowing that mercy could flow from terrible strength.
